title: Heatwave leaves national capital’s wildlife gasping - The Tribune
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Delhi
full_text:
Heatwave leaves national capital’s wildlife gasping
Rescue calls surged by 40 per cent in April, says Wildlife SOS data
The scorching summer that has pushed temperature across Delhi-NCR to uncomfortable highs is taking a severe toll on the region’s wildlife as well.
Conservationists say birds, reptiles and mammals are increasingly being found dehydrated, exhausted and stranded in urban spaces as they struggle to cope with rising temperature and shrinking natural habitats.
According to the rescue data released by the Wildlife SOS, the organisation witnessed a nearly 40 per cent rise in wildlife rescue cases across Delhi-NCR in April this year compared to the same month in 2025. The spike was most pronounced among birds, with rescue calls increasing by almost 49 per cent, while cases involving mammals and reptiles rose by about 24 per cent.
The figures are significant because April, traditionally considered a relatively moderate month, is increasingly emerging as a period of intense wildlife distress due to early-season heatwaves.
The Wildlife SOS said its rapid response unit is currently attending to over 10 heat-related distress calls every day. Black kites, pigeons, parakeets, owls and peafowls are among the most frequently rescued birds. Teams have also rescued rhesus macaques, squirrels, bats, civets, monitor lizards, cobras and rat snakes from various parts of the Capital.
Rescuers report finding animals collapsed on roadsides, trapped inside office complexes, railway stations and residential colonies, or unable to fly due to dehydration and heat exhaustion. Many are believed to have ventured into urban areas in search of water and shade.
Experts attribute the trend to a combination of extreme weather and rapid urbanisation. Large stretches of concrete, asphalt roads and glass-fronted buildings create heat islands that absorb and radiate heat, making conditions even harsher for wildlife. As natural water sources dry up and green cover declines, animals are forced into unfamiliar surroundings where they face additional risks.
“The rising number of heat-related rescues shows how extreme weather is changing the way wildlife survives in cities,” said Kartick Satyanarayan, co-founder and CEO of Wildlife SOS. He noted that animals are increasingly being found in unusual locations as they desperately search for water and shelter.
Wasim Akram, Director, sustainability and special projects, Wildlife SOS, said protecting urban biodiversity requires preserving green spaces and ensuring access to water, especially during prolonged heatwaves.
The organisation has urged residents to place bowls of clean water in shaded areas, protect trees and green spaces and report injured or distressed wildlife to the authorised rescue agencies.
Conservationists say such simple measures can make a crucial difference as Delhi’s summers grow hotter and more unpredictable.

title: Temp falls by as much as 19°C in some places; ‘orange’ alert issued in Delhi | Latest News Delhi - Hindustan Times
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Delhi
full_text:
Temp falls by as much as 19°C in some places; ‘orange’ alert issued in Delhi
Thunderstorms and strong winds cool Delhi after scorching day, plunging temperatures by 9°C. IMD issues orange alert as rain hits city, air quality worsens.
After a scorching day that saw the mercury touch 40.5°C, thunderstorms, rain and strong winds cooled large parts of Delhi on Thursday evening, Thunderstorms and gusty winds touching 91kmph caused temperatures to plunge by 9°C to 19°C in parts of Delhi within hours.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) upgraded a yellow alert issued for the city in the morning to an orange alert around 7pm as thunderstorm activity intensified across the region.

Ayanagar, Mayur Vihar record highest rainfall
Till 5.30pm, Ayanagar recorded the highest rainfall at 27.6mm, followed by Mayur Vihar at 24.5mm and Ridge at 19mm. Pragati Maidan received 12.8mm of rain, Palam 10.7mm, Pusa 8mm, Lodhi Road 4mm and Najafgarh 1.5mm. Safdarjung, considered representative of Delhi’s weather, recorded only 1.6mm of rainfall, while Pitampura received 0.5mm.
IMD data showed a sharp drop in temperatures in several areas following the thunderstorms. Ayanagar recorded the steepest decline, with temperatures falling 19°C from 41°C to 22.7°C. Dadri recorded a drop of 15.7°C, while Pushp Vihar and Mayur Vihar saw temperatures fall by around 14°C to 15°C. At Safdarjung, the temperature dropped 9°C from 40°C to 31°C.
Strong winds accompanied the rain across much of the city and adjoining areas. The highest wind speed was recorded at Dadri at 91kmph, followed by Palam at 65kmph. Safdarjung recorded a maximum wind speed of 36kmph.

Despite rains, temperature high
Despite the evening rain, Delhi’s maximum temperature for the day stood at 40.5°C, while the minimum temperature was recorded at 29.7°C.
The IMD has issued a yellow alert for Friday, forecasting multiple spells of light rain and thunderstorms along with a further decline in both maximum and minimum temperatures.
Rain is expected to continue on Saturday as well, before temperatures begin rising again from Sunday. The maximum temperature is likely to reach 40°C to 42°C by June 9.
Meanwhile, Delhi’s air quality deteriorated despite the rainfall. The city’s 24-hour average Air Quality Index (AQI) rose by 21 points from 143 on Wednesday to 164 on Thursday, remaining in the “moderate” category.

title: Delhi LG reviews heatwave relief with cooling zones across eight districts - The News Mill
full_text:
Delhi Lieutenant Governor Taranjit Singh Sandhu reviewed relief operations and preventive measures addressing the ongoing heatwave in the national capital on Thursday evening. The Divisional Commissioner and other concerned officers attended the meeting, according to a press note.
The LG was informed that the Revenue Department, GNCTD, had established several dedicated Cooling Zones across eight districts at various locations. Mobile Heat Relief Units (MHRU) have been deployed since May 6, 2026, actively providing relief and essential support to citizens in all 13 districts, officials said.
On Tuesday, the Delhi government intensified preparations for the heatwave, with Health Minister Dr Pankaj Kumar Singh inspecting arrangements at Lok Nayak Jai Prakash (LNJP) Hospital. During his visit, he examined emergency care systems, ICU facilities, medical staff readiness, and patient management infrastructure.
Dr Singh stated that the health department and medical teams continuously monitor the situation to prevent any inconvenience due to extreme heat. He appealed to residents to avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight and to seek immediate medical attention if experiencing symptoms such as weakness, dizziness, dehydration, fever, or heat-related illness.
He concluded, “Delhi’s healthcare system is fully prepared to deal with every challenge posed by the heatwave. Our priority is to ensure that no citizen faces any difficulty during this period of extreme heat.”
To protect personnel working under severe conditions, the department launched a high-tech relief initiative, introducing air-conditioned helmets and portable fans for officers managing busy intersections. This cooling headgear is being piloted for traffic personnel stationed for extended periods in direct sunlight.
Assistant Sub-Inspector Veer Singh described the technology as “a game-changer. The helmets keep officers cool and relaxed throughout their shift.” He said, “This helmet keeps us relaxed in this weather. It can work easily for 4-5 hours, depending on the charge in its battery…”
The cooling system operates effectively for 4-5 hours, providing sustained comfort during peak heat. For those overseeing VIP movements, mobile vans have been deployed as hydration and nutrition hubs to supply essential water and food, preventing exhaustion.
The initiative coincides with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issuing Red and Orange alerts across multiple states, signalling a period of severe, life-threatening temperatures.

title: Delhi government sets up special ward at LNJP hospital for heatwave patients - The News Mill
full_text:
The Delhi government has enhanced its efforts to address the ongoing heatwave by establishing a dedicated “Heat Related Illness” ward at Lok Nayak Jai Prakash (LNJP) Hospital, Health Minister Dr Pankaj Kumar Singh announced on Tuesday.
During a visit to the hospital, Dr Singh reviewed the emergency care systems, ICU facilities, preparedness of medical staff, and patient management infrastructure. He stated that the health department and medical teams are continuously monitoring the situation to prevent any inconvenience to citizens caused by the extreme heat.
The newly created ward at LNJP Hospital is equipped with timely treatment facilities, dedicated beds, emergency care support, ICU backup, and step-down recovery arrangements for patients affected by heatwave conditions.
“The Delhi Health Department is fully prepared. If any problem arises, be it heat stroke or a disaster, all our doctors in the hospitals, our staff, everyone is ready for it… It’s a very happy thing that our doctors and our staff are eager in the direction of treating patients,” Dr Singh said.
He added that patients experiencing dehydration, high fever, heat exhaustion, and other heat-related complications are treated according to medical need, with critical cases transferred promptly to emergency or ICU care.
Dr Singh confirmed that adequate beds, ICU facilities, and emergency response systems are in place to manage any potential surge in patients. Medical staff, including doctors, specialists, ICU personnel, and healthcare workers, are fully prepared to handle heatwave-related emergencies.
The minister emphasised that preparedness extends beyond LNJP Hospital, with all government hospitals, polyclinics, and Ayushman Arogya Mandirs across Delhi placed on alert with necessary arrangements.
Under Chief Minister Rekha Gupta’s leadership, mobile relief units and outreach services have been deployed to various areas, providing oral rehydration solutions (ORS), cold drinking water, and other essential support services.
Dr Singh appealed to residents to avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight and extreme heat, urging anyone experiencing symptoms such as weakness, dizziness, dehydration, fever, or heat-related illness to seek immediate medical attention.
He concluded, “Delhi’s healthcare system is fully prepared to deal with every challenge posed by the heatwave. Our priority is to ensure that no citizen faces any difficulty during this period of extreme heat.”

title: Delhi gets relief from heatwave as rain brings down temperatures; Thunderstorm alert issued for Delhi, Noida, Gurugram, Ghaziabad, Faridabad till… - news24online.com
full_text:
Delhi gets relief from heatwave as rain brings down temperatures; Thunderstorm alert issued for Delhi, Noida, Gurugram, Ghaziabad, Faridabad till…
Delhi received rain, thunderstorms and strong winds, bringing relief from intense heat. Temperatures dropped by 3-4°C, while IMD issued a yellow alert for more rain.
The weather in Delhi has changed significantly during the first week of June. Heavy rain, thunderstorms and strong winds on Thursday brought much-needed relief from the intense heat. With the monsoon arriving in Kerala and a weather system affecting northern India, temperatures across Delhi-NCR have dropped noticeably. According to the India Meteorological Department (IMD), residents can expect relief from severe heat for at least the next three days.
How Much temperature has dropped?
Light to moderate rain fell across Delhi on Thursday afternoon and evening, while some areas experienced strong winds. As a result, the maximum temperature dropped by around 3 to 4 degrees Celsius. Temperatures that were previously hovering between 40°C and 41°C are now expected to remain between 36°C and 38°C. Minimum temperatures are likely to stay between 23°C and 25°C. In some parts of Delhi-NCR, temperatures fell as low as 19°C.
Has the Rain Caused Any Damage?
While the cooler weather has been welcomed by residents, the stormy conditions also caused damage in some areas. Several trees were uprooted and fell onto roads in Noida and Ghaziabad, disrupting traffic. Despite this, many people were relieved to see an end to the uncomfortable heat and humidity that had affected the region in recent weeks.
What Is Causing the Change in Weather?
Weather experts say the change has been caused by a western disturbance, along with a cyclonic circulation over parts of Pakistan, Haryana and north-west Rajasthan. These weather systems have brought cloud cover, rain and thunderstorms to Delhi-NCR, making days more pleasant and nights cooler.
How Much Rain Fell Across Delhi?
Ayanagar recorded the highest rainfall at 27.8 mm. Other rainfall figures include:
What Is the Forecast for the Next Two Days?
The IMD has issued a yellow alert for Delhi-NCR. On Friday and Saturday, light to moderate rain, thunderstorms and gusty winds are expected. Wind speeds could reach between 40 and 60 km/h. For Friday, the sky is expected to remain partly cloudy, with one or two spells of rain and thunderstorms possible. Maximum temperatures are likely to stay between 36°C and 38°C, while minimum temperatures may range from 23°C to 25°C.
Will the Weather Stay Pleasant Over the Weekend?
Saturday is expected to bring partly cloudy skies and the possibility of light rain. However, from Sunday onwards, the weather is likely to become clearer and temperatures may begin rising again. Mahesh Palawat, Vice-President of Skymet Weather, said that the western disturbance and cyclonic circulation are likely to keep rain and thunderstorm activity active across Delhi-NCR and nearby areas for the next three days.
What Is the Current Air Quality Like?
According to the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), Delhi's 24-hour average Air Quality Index (AQI) stood at 164, which falls under the "Moderate" category.

title: 100 Suspected Sunstroke Deaths In May, Cases Under Audit Of Govt Panel - The Times of India
full_text:
NAGPUR: With nearly 100 suspected sunstroke-related deaths in May under review of the district death audit committee, Nagpur witnessed one of its harshest summers in recent years, underscoring the growing impact of extreme weather events on public health.
Among the most disturbing indicators of the season's severity was the death of 98 unidentified persons on the streets in May, many of whom are suspected to have succumbed to heat-related illnesses, health officials told TOI.
Such deaths are reviewed by the district death audit committee comprising civil surgeon, medical officer (health) of Nagpur Municipal Corporation (NMC), public health officials and medical college doctors before confirming heatstroke. Health officials said deaths of unidentified persons during peak summer afternoons are treated as suspected cases until complete histopathological investigation reports are available.
"These take two months to be tabled. The reports before the death audit panel which can only confirm heatstroke," they said. However, civic officials said it becomes difficult to pursue autopsy reports due to absence of relatives and thus it takes time.
The alarming toll came into sharp focus on the eve of World Environment Day, raising concerns over rising temperatures, urban heat stress and the increasing vulnerability of people living on the margins.
Most of the deceased were homeless and destitute living on pavements, under flyovers and in open spaces, with no or limited access to shelter, drinking water or medical care. The crisis intensified during the final week of May. Records show that 31 bodies of unknown persons were reported within just two days, 16 on May 27 and 15 on May 28.
In another alarming episode, 11 suspected heatstroke deaths were recorded within a single 24-hour period, reflecting the deadly impact of prolonged exposure to extreme temperatures. These 11 included those found dead in unreserved coaches of trains passing through Nagpur.
According to data, 59 unidentified natural deaths were reported at Indira Gandhi Govt Medical College and Hospital while 39 deaths were recorded at Govt Medical College and Hospital. Besides these 98, a couple of deaths of labourers from suspected sunstroke were recorded in peri-urban areas in March and April.
Nagpur experienced exceptionally harsh weather throughout May, with temperatures repeatedly touching 45°C to 46.5°C for more than 12 days. Some parts of Vidarbha like Bramhapuri and Wardha recorded temperatures of 47°C. This year was marked by unusually prolonged and intense heatwave conditions.
IMD scientist Pravin Kumar told TOI that May normally records 5 to 6 heatwave days. "This time the number doubled. It was also the driest phase for the region in the absence of rainfall," he said.
Medical experts warn that sustained exposure to such temperatures can trigger dehydration, heatstroke, electrolyte imbalance, kidney failure, cardiovascular stress and multi-organ complications, particularly among vulnerable populations. Many of the victims are believed to have suffered from a combination of these conditions.
The deaths are currently classified as suspected heatstroke fatalities and are undergoing detailed medical audits. Hospitals also reported an unusual surge in unclaimed bodies during the heatwave, highlighting how extreme weather disproportionately affects the homeless, elderly and socially isolated.
The impact extended beyond Nagpur city. Earlier official figures documented 63 suspected sunstroke deaths across the Nagpur division, including Chandrapur, Wardha, Bhandara, Gondia and Gadchiroli, before the toll rose further.

title: गर्मी से बेहाल दिल्ली-NCR को मिलेगी राहत, शुक्रवार को भी आंधी-बारिश का अलर्ट - Jagran
search_term: (सूर्याघात OR तापाघात OR "लू लगना" OR "लू से मौत" OR "गर्मी से मौत" OR "हीट स्ट्रोक" OR "सन स्ट्रोक" OR "लू से मरे" OR निर्जलीकरण OR डिहाइड्रेशन OR "अस्पताल में भर्ती" OR "गर्मी से बेहाल" OR "गर्मी से त्राहि-त्राहि") Delhi
full_text:
गर्मी से बेहाल दिल्ली-NCR को मिलेगी राहत, शुक्रवार को भी आंधी-बारिश का अलर्ट
दिल्ली में पश्चिमी विक्षोभ और साइक्लोनिक सर्कुलेशन के कारण तेज आंधी के साथ झमाझम बारिश हुई, जिससे दो घंटे में तापमान 19 डिग्री तक गिर गया। ...और पढ़ें
HighLights
दिल्ली में पश्चिमी विक्षोभ से तेज आंधी और बारिश हुई।
दो घंटे में तापमान 19 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक गिर गया।
मौसम विभाग ने शुक्रवार के लिए भी येलो अलर्ट जारी किया।
राज्य ब्यूरो, नई दिल्ली। यलो अलर्ट के बीच बृहस्पतिवार को दिल्ली के मौसम ने एक बार फिर करवट ले ली। पश्चिमी विक्षोभ और साइक्लोनिक सर्कुलेशन के चलते 65 किमी प्रति घंटे की रफ्तार वाली तेज आंधी के साथ झमाझम वर्षा हुई तो दो घंटे में तापमान 19 डिग्री तक गिर गया।
हालांकि ये स्थिति केवल आंधी- वर्षा के दौरान ही थी। समग्र रूप से दिल्ली का तापमान 40 डिग्री के पार रहा। मौसम विभाग ने शुक्रवार को भी कमोबेश ऐसा ही मौसम बने रहने एवं तेज आंधी वर्षा का यलो अलर्ट जारी किया है।
बृहस्पतिवार को सुबह सूरज अपने तय समय पर ही निकल गया था। दिन चढ़ने के साथ साथ धूप भी तेज होती रही। उमस भरी गर्मी से हाल बेहाल रहा। दोपहर एक बजे के लगभग आसमान बादलों से घिर गया और एकाएक काली घटा भी छा गई। फिर देखते ही देखते पहले धूल भरी तेज आंधी चली और उसके बाद तेज वर्षा शुरू हो गई। कहीं तेज तो कहीं हल्की वर्षा का यह दौर रुक रुककर शाम तक चलता रहा।
मौसम विभाग के आंकड़ों के मुताबिक इस दौरान कई इलाकों में महज दो घंटे के भीतर तापमान 9 से 19 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक गिर गया। सबसे ज्यादा राहत दक्षिणी दिल्ली के अयानगर इलाके में दर्ज की गई, जहां तापमान 41.7 डिग्री से गिरकर 22.7 डिग्री पर पहुंच गया। यानी सिर्फ दो घंटे में 19 डिग्री की गिरावट रिकार्ड हुई। धूल भरी आंधी की सर्वाधिक 65 किमी प्रति घंटे की रफ़्तार पालम में दर्ज की गई।
बृहस्पतिवार को दिल्ली का अधिकतम तापमान सामान्य से 0.5 डिग्री अधिक 40.5 डिग्री सेल्सियस दर्ज किया गया। वहीं न्यूनतम तापमान सामान्य से 2.8 डिग्री अधिक 29.7 डिग्री सेल्सियस दर्ज हुआ। हवा में नमी का स्तर 65 से 37 प्रतिशत रहा। वर्षा रात साढ़े आठ बजे तक 1.6 मिमी दर्ज की गई। सर्वाधिक 25.0 मिमी वर्षा मयूर विहार में दर्ज हुई।
मौसम विभाग ने शुक्रवार के लिए भी ऐसा ही मौसम बने रहने का पूर्वानुमान जताते हुए यलो अलर्ट भी जारी किया है। दिन भर बादल छाए रहेंगे। गर्जन वाले बादल बनने, बिजली चमकने और 50 से 60 किमी प्रति घंटे तक की रफ्तार वाली तेज हवाओं के साथ दिनभर में वर्षा के कई होने का अनुमान है। अधिकतम तापमान 37 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रह सकता है।

title: Delhi Weather: दिल्ली को अगले 3 दिन तक गर्मी से राहत, बारिश और तेज हवाओं का दौर, गिरेगा तापमान - ABP News
search_term: ("हीट एक्शन प्लान" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR चेतावनी OR "रेड अलर्ट" OR "ऑरेंज अलर्ट" OR "येलो अलर्ट" OR एडवाइजरी OR "राहत सामग्री" OR "राहत शिविर" OR मुआवजा) Delhi
full_text:
Delhi Weather: दिल्ली को अगले 3 दिन तक गर्मी से राहत, बारिश और तेज हवाओं का दौर, गिरेगा तापमान
Delhi Weather Update: आज शुक्रवार (5 जून) को न्यूनतम तापमान 23-25 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच और अधिकतम तापमान 36.38 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच रहने की संभावना है. इस दौरान आसमान बादलों से घिरा रह सकता है.
- गुरुवार को दिल्ली-एनसीआर में आंधी-बारिश, भीषण गर्मी से राहत मिली.
- आंधी-बारिश से पेड़ गिरे, दीवारें ढहीं; रेड अलर्ट जारी था.
- आज शुक्रवार को हल्की बारिश, गरज के साथ तेज हवाएँ संभव.
दिल्ली-एनसीआर कई दिनों से जारी भीषण गर्मी के प्रकोप के बाद गुरुवार को लोगों को बड़ी राहत मिली. दिल्ली-एनसीआर में गुरुवार (4 जून) को अधिकतम तापमान 40.5 डिग्री सेल्सियस और न्यूनतम तापमान 29.7 डिग्री सेल्सियस दर्ज किया गया. यहां के कई इलाकों में अचानक तेज आंधी और तूफान के साथ भारी बारिश देखने को मिली. इस दौरान पेड़ गिरने और दीवार ढहने की घटनाएं सामने आईं. हालांकि, किसी के हताहत होने की कोई सूचना नहीं आई.
मौसम में आए इस आचानक बदलाव के कारण कई जगह जाम भी देखने को मिला. भारतीय मौसम विभाग (IMD) ने पहले ही यहां रेड अलर्ट जारी करते हुए खराब मौसम की चेतावनी दी थी. मौसम विशेषज्ञों के अनुसार, पश्चिमी विक्षोभ और स्थानीय मौसमी गतिविधियों के कारण मौसम में बदलाव देखने को मिला है. आज भी दिल्ली में बारिश और तेज आंधी की संभावनाएं जताई गईं हैं.
कैसा रहा पिछले 24 घंटो का तापमान?
पिछले 24 घंटों के दौरान दिल्ली में न्यूनतम तापमान 27-30 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच और अधिकतम तापमान 39-41 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच दर्ज किया गया. पिछले 24 घंटों के दौरान दिल्ली में अधिकतम तापमान में 1 डिग्री सेल्सियस की मामूली बढ़ोतरी दर्ज की गई और न्यूनतम तापमान में 2-3 डिग्री सेल्सियस की बढ़ोतरी हुई. इस दौरान दिल्ली में दक्षिण-पूर्वी हवाएं चलीं, जिनकी गति 10-15 किमी/घंटा रही और झोंको के साथ 60 किमी/घंटा तक पहुंच गई.
कैसा रहेगा आज दिल्ली का तापमान?
मौसम विभाग के अनुसार, आज शुक्रवार (5 जून) को न्यूनतम तापमान 23-25 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच और अधिकतम तापमान 36.38 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच रहने की संभावना है. IMD के अनुसार, आज आसमान आमतौर पर बादलों से घिरा रहेगा. सुबह से दोपहर तक हल्की बारिश, गरज-चमक के साथ तेज हवाओं का एक दौर आ सकता है. इस दौरान हवा की गति 40-50 किमी/घंटा तक पहुंच सकती है, और झोंकों के साथ दोपहर या शाम के समय में हवा की गति 60 किमी/घंटा तक जा सकती है. शाम से रात के समय में भी हल्की बारिश का एक दौर आ सकता है.
अगले 3 दिनों का तापमान कैसा रहेगा?
IMD के अनुसार, दिल्ली में अगले 3 दिनों के दौरान अधिकतम तापमान में 3-4 डिग्री सेल्सियस की गिरावट आने की संभावना है. इसके बाद तापमान में 3-4 डिग्री सेल्सियस की बढ़ोतरी का पूर्वानुमान है. मौसम विभाग का पूर्वानुमान है कि अगले 24 घंटों के दौरान न्यूनतम तापमान में लगभग 2 डिग्री सेल्सियस की गिरावट आ सकती है. इसके बाद अगले 24 घंटों में कोई बड़ा बदलाव नहीं होगा, जबकि उसके बाद धीरे-धीरे 2-3 डिग्री सेल्सियस की बढ़ोतरी हो सकती है. इससे सुबह और रात के समय मौसम थोड़ा सुहावना बना रह सकता है.
